title: C++ Introduction and Syntax
description: Learn the fundamentals of C++ programming, including what C++ is, how it works, and the basic syntax for writing C++ programs.
learningObjectives: Understand what C++ is and its purpose in programming
Learn C++ syntax structure and program structure
Write and compile a basic C++ program
Understand variables and data types in C++

subtopics.title: What is C++?
subtopics.content:
C++ is a powerful, high-performance programming language created by Bjarne Stroustrup. It's an extension of the C programming language with object-oriented features.

**Key Points:**
- Compiled language (converts to machine code)
- Supports multiple programming paradigms
- Used for system software, game development, and high-performance applications
- Gives low-level memory control

**Why Use C++?**
- **Performance**: Fast execution and efficient memory use
- **Control**: Direct memory manipulation
- **Object-Oriented**: Supports classes and objects
- **Portability**: Code runs on many platforms

**Common Uses:**
- Operating systems (Windows, Linux)
- Game engines (Unreal Engine)
- Embedded systems
- High-performance applications

**C++ Version History:**
- C++98 (1998) - First standard
- C++11 (2011) - Major update with modern features
- C++14, C++17, C++20 - Incremental improvements
- C++23 - Latest standard
commonMisconceptions: C++ is NOT just C with classes - it has many modern features beyond object-oriented programming.
C++ is NOT obsolete - it's still widely used in performance-critical applications.
You do NOT need to know C before learning C++ - you can learn C++ directly.
subtopics.realWorldAnalogy: C++ is like a high-performance sports car. It gives you complete control over every aspect of the engine (memory management), can go extremely fast (performance), but requires more skill to drive safely compared to automatic cars (higher-level languages).

subtopics.title: C++ Program Structure
commonMisconceptions: using namespace std; is NOT required - you can use std::cout instead, but it's more verbose.
main() does NOT have to return 0 - but it's good practice to return a success status.
Comments do NOT affect program performance - they're completely ignored by the compiler.
subtopics.realWorldAnalogy: C++ program structure is like a letter with a standard format. The #include is like attaching reference documents, using namespace is like setting a default address format, main() is the actual letter content, and return 0 is like signing off with 'sincerely'.

subtopics.title: Variables and Data Types
subtopics.content:
C++ has several built-in data types for storing different kinds of data.

**Basic Data Types:**
```cpp
int age = 25;           // Integer
float price = 19.99;    // Floating-point
double pi = 3.14159;    // Double precision
char grade = 'A';        // Single character
bool isPassed = true;    // Boolean
string name = "John";    // String (needs #include <string>)
```

**Integer Types:**
- `int` - Standard integer (usually 4 bytes)
- `short` - Short integer (2 bytes)
- `long` - Long integer (4 or 8 bytes)
- `long long` - Very long integer (8 bytes)

**Unsigned Types:**
```cpp
unsigned int x = 40000;  // Only positive numbers
unsigned char c = 255;
```

**Variable Declaration:**
```cpp
int x;              // Declaration
x = 10;            // Assignment
int y = 20;         // Initialization
const int MAX = 100; // Constant (cannot change)
```
commonMisconceptions: int size is NOT always 4 bytes - it depends on the system and compiler.
float and double are NOT the same - double has more precision and is generally preferred.
char stores ONE character, not a string - use string type for text.
subtopics.realWorldAnalogy: Data types are like different container sizes. int is a standard box, short is a small box, long long is a large box, and float/double are boxes for decimal numbers. You choose the right container size for what you need to store.

subtopics.title: Input and Output
commonMisconceptions: cin does NOT read spaces in input - it stops at whitespace, use getline() for full lines.
endl does NOT just add a newline - it also flushes the buffer, which can be slower.
cout and cin are NOT functions - they are objects of the iostream class.
subtopics.realWorldAnalogy: Input and output streams are like pipes. cout is a pipe sending data out to the screen (like water flowing out of a faucet), and cin is a pipe bringing data in from the keyboard (like water flowing into a container).

subtopics.title: Operators
subtopics.content:
C++ supports various operators for performing operations.

**Arithmetic Operators:**
```cpp
int a = 10, b = 3;
int sum = a + b;      // 13
int diff = a - b;     // 7
int product = a * b;  // 30
int quotient = a / b; // 3 (integer division)
int remainder = a % b; // 1 (modulo)
```

**Increment/Decrement:**
```cpp
int x = 5;
x++;      // x = 6 (post-increment)
++x;      // x = 7 (pre-increment)
x--;      // x = 6 (post-decrement)
--x;      // x = 5 (pre-decrement)
```

**Comparison Operators:**
```cpp
int a = 5, b = 10;
a == b;   // false (equal)
a != b;   // true (not equal)
a < b;    // true (less than)
a > b;    // false (greater than)
a <= b;   // true (less than or equal)
a >= b;   // false (greater than or equal)
```

**Logical Operators:**
```cpp
bool a = true, b = false;
a && b;   // false (AND)
a || b;   // true (OR)
!a;       // false (NOT)
```
commonMisconceptions: = is assignment, == is comparison - they are NOT the same and using = in conditions is a common bug.
Integer division truncates the decimal - 10/3 = 3, not 3.33.
Pre-increment (++x) and post-increment (x++) have different behavior when used in expressions.
subtopics.realWorldAnalogy: Operators are like mathematical symbols and comparison words. Arithmetic operators are calculator buttons (+, -, ×, ÷), comparison operators are like comparison words (greater than, less than, equal to), and logical operators are like combining conditions (and, or, not).

recommendedTools.name: Online GDB
recommendedTools.reason: Online C++ compiler for testing code

furtherReading: The C++ Programming Language by Bjarne Stroustrup

glossary.definition: A high-performance programming language that extends C with object-oriented features.
A program that converts C++ source code into machine code that can be executed.
A named storage location in memory that holds a value.
The type of data a variable can hold (int, float, char, bool, etc.).
Standard output stream in C++ used to display output to the console.
Standard input stream in C++ used to get input from the user.
A declarative region that provides a scope to identifiers.
The entry point of a C++ program where execution begins.
